plan.narration_reading: The cut opens on the empty tomb under 'stop reading Psalm 22 at the cross' — the visual rebuttal to ending in a grave. The Psalm-scroll and the David-to-Christ diptych carry 'the psalm turns / anguish to praise'; the fingertip-under-Hebrew lands exactly on 'I will declare thy name', and Christ among the gathered on 'in the midst of the congregation'. The living face asks 'who is that voice?', the Greek codex carries 'Hebrews takes that opening line', and the risen Christ standing alive lands 'in the mouth of the risen Christ'; the scarred hands in praise land 'alive on the far side of the cross'. The embrace, the hand-on-shoulder, and the welcomed-family beat the 'not ashamed / brothers / family' run; the inviting face closes the spoken brethren line, and hero #10 (arms opening to the viewer) holds the final 'calling you into that family'.
plan.red_team_notes: Several risen-Christ faces are close in look (#04/#06/#11) — I spread them across distinct beats (reveal / question / invitation) so they don't read as repeats. #12 (diptych) is the only OT-echo image and sits on 'the psalm turns', which is its exact meaning. Risk: P21 has no beat clip so the hero must land cleanly on the closing line — acceptable since hero is the held still close.

slots.rationale: Empty tomb = the most arresting hook-open; the visual answer to 'it doesn't end in a grave', pinned to beat 0.
slots.beat_phrase: Most people stop reading Psalm twenty-two at the cross.

slots.rationale: The Psalm scroll, lower lines breaking into light, on 'Later, the psalm turns'.
David-to-risen-Christ diptych across a band of light = 'the same voice moves from anguish to praise' across a thousand years.

Scarred hand on the man's shoulder on 'brothers' — brotherhood made tangible.

slots.beat_phrase: and the risen Christ is not ashamed to call His own brethren.

slots.rationale: Hero close — the cut lands on Christ (single appearance, no reuse).

panel.note: 13 distinct moments tile 58s contiguously; brisk viral pace with two breathing holds (#02, #11) — flows, does not strobe.
Every clip sits on its phrase: scroll on 'the psalm turns', fingertip on 'I will declare thy name', codex on 'Hebrews takes that line', embrace on 'not ashamed to call them brethren'.
13 distinct body clips; hero #10 appears only at the close.
Avg 1.34x is brisk for slow Baroque; hero #10 held at 1.0x and #11 near-full speed keep the sacred landing reverent — acceptable for a fast Short.
Opens on the empty tomb (scroll-stopper), closes on the risen Christ with arms open to the viewer — lands on Jesus, not a merely emotional frame.
Empty-tomb open + the death->resurrection->family turn earns the stop; seams are tight.

gates.evidence: Thread carried open (empty tomb) -> climax (risen Christ / scarred hands) -> close (calling you in); no clip contradicts its words.

slots.words: Most people stop reading Psalm twenty-two at the cross.
slots.note: Empty rock-hewn tomb with burial ledge and dawn light, no figure — matches the empty-tomb hook.
Open Hebrew Psalm scroll, lower lines catching light, faint background vignettes — matches 'the psalm turns'.

slots.words: the same voice moves from anguish to praise:
slots.note: Aged David with scroll lifting his face in hope, risen Christ in white at the divide — matches the anguish-to-praise turn.

slots.words: "I will declare thy name unto my brethren:
slots.note: Weathered fingertip beneath a line of ancient Hebrew on cracked parchment — matches 'I will declare thy name'.

slots.words: in the midst of the congregation will I praise thee."
slots.note: Risen Christ at the centre of a gathered group of men and women, hand lifted — matches 'in the midst of the congregation'.
Close view of the risen Christ's living eyes and the faint thorn-scar at the brow — matches 'who is that praising voice?'.
Macro of an ancient codex / manuscript edge — the book under 'Hebrews takes that opening line'; relevant, no foreign story.

slots.words: and puts it in the mouth of the risen Christ.
slots.note: The risen Christ, living bearded face, white robe — matches 'in the mouth of the risen Christ'.

slots.words: alive on the far side of the cross.
slots.note: Two open palms with healed nail-marks raised, the living face above — matches 'alive on the far side of the cross'.
Risen Christ drawing a kneeling man into an embrace, hand on his shoulder — matches 'not ashamed to call them brethren'.
The risen Christ's scarred open hand, healed nail-mark visible, resting on a weathered man's shoulder — matches 'brothers'.
Hands reaching to welcome, meeting an open hand among the gathered — matches 'Family'.

slots.words: and the risen Christ is not ashamed to call His own brethren.
slots.note: Risen Christ's living face turned warmly to the viewer — matches 'the risen Christ is not ashamed to call His own brethren'.
